item.content:encoded:
Always happy to give a milestone!

The cracked article linked on page 1 of the discussion scared me.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Due to this, the second and third images have been removed.

item.title: Image sources
item.content:encoded:
image 1 is just molecular diagrams. Public domain because too simple to be copyrighted.

image 2 is Ophiopogon planiscapus 'Nigrescens', "black mondo grass". Tineye shows that versions of this exact pic have been floating around for a while; I can't trace its provenance, so we can't assume it's Creative Commons, but it's easily replaceable.

image 3 is an example of Apiosporina morbosa, the Black Knot Fungus, growing on a Black Cherry tree (Prunus serotina). This particular photo is copyright of the Hilton Pond Center, in York, South Carolina, and may not be used without their permission. http://www.hiltonpond.org/ThisWeek031201.html Again, easily replaceable.

edit: correction, the black grass appears to come from Loveland Living Planet Aquarium, in Draper, Utah - https://thelivingplanet.com/black-mondo-grass/ (it looks to me like all their photos are taken on-site). Copyrighted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm, sciencey…

I didn't catch the first version of this, but this one is excellent.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. That took me way too long to figure out…

Weeelll… there's possible explanations: The Other Wiki

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… HA!

Anyone mind if I leave that in? It amuses me, now that I had to drag my tired face to google to figure out what's such a big deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a second...

02/30/2013- Vector design is stalled. All test carriers degrade int[UNRECOVERABLE]

02/30

aka February 30

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… y'know, everyone loves to talk about H.P. Lovecraft and all his fangorious gelatinous beasties from beyond the veil past the darkness behind the stars (or what James Wilson so famously called his "invisible whistling octopus"). The entirety of the Cthulhu Mythos is easily the thing he and his circle were most famous for. And yet, much of a fan as I am of Lovecraft, my favorite stories of his have always been the ones that border on the plausible. Cool Air, Re-Animator, these were the tales that stayed with me, the stories that were just about ordinary people striving for discovery and knowledge and new science.

That is just the sort of story that you've woven for us here. Take my upvote, with my congratulations and my eager hope for more to come.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Err… the next line or do says to incinerate all remains. Including mulch. Mulching breaks up the wood to ensure a thorough burn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Changed my vote from neutral to +1. This is *much* better with the ad bit as part of the main 'recovered stuff' log. The science is fantastic, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Trees infected with the fungal vector for SCP-1462 are to be removed and mulched. Other infected specimens are to be removed and destroyed.

If you mulch something you aren't going to destroy the fungi. You are going to reintroduce the spores to a compost pile…which will be distributed to fertilize fields. I know that you used the word "mulch" to change it up from being "destroyed/terminated/whatever" but really you should incinerate this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. The science is good, the effects believable, and the ad is what I would expect from An advertising perspective. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed for all these reasons and a few more.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how the footnotes tell a story if you just read them in order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone living in a city with record-breaking wildfires 2 years in a row, It's an EXTREMELY scary thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted hard

Very believeable science, with ample details and a good lead in for testing and data collection in either future logs, or in tales. I absolutely adore "mundane" SCPs. We have enough magic, but we need more things like this, with things that are so absolutely abnormal that they couldn't exist, but "real" enough that you could believe that it possibly might.

Now excuse me while I go write a tale about this…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite having just had a fungal SCP released, this one works out quite well. It gets more terrifying when you look at the year the logs stop and think about all the wildfires that have been happening out West lately…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked, have a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Writing is good, believable to someone like me who doesn't understand science. I dare say you went on about science for a little bit too long and it turned into a why am I reading this who cares ordeal. I like the pictures too.

This is boring. Turns stuff into fuel, some people died. Okay.

Downvoted according to my "If this was the first article on the site I read, would I ever come back again?" rule. I would not come back. I would probably go browse YTMND and eat rice cakes or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The science here is fantastic. This is a very believeable SCP. I'm really quite fond of this, since it operates on purely realistic principles. As a scientist, my favorite detail is the pheromone. That's a clever and logical way to trigger the burn cycle that operates well within real scientific behavior. It's a minor detail, but it's extremely satisfying to see a solid explanation rather than the more common mysterious action method.

All the +1s

item.title: This seems very familiar to me...
item.content:encoded:
I did a little bit of Google-Fu, and found this gem that I'd probably read a couple of years ago: http://www.cracked.com/article_18503_how-biotech-company-almost-killed-world-with-booze.html

Short version: A biotech firm sets up a strain of bacteria to turn dead plant matter into alcohol, and prepares to release it in the wild. Turns out that the bacteria doesn't just work on dead plant matter. Not to mention the bacteria that the company derived it from used one that was both omnipresent and aggressive. Yeah, it's likely we'd have wrecked the biosphere with that one, and they did all of the required testing, to boot.

Anyway… no vote for the moment. Not sure about things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, really cool concept.

Hi, [DATA EXPUNGE] here, to tell you about the new, all-natural GYO-Biofuel..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a researcher's log- I didn't realize people seemed more interested in what the ad suggested than some of the sciencey stuff. Please let me know how it flows!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda love this, but the ad is weird. It kinda seems like it's saying that the virus is from the future.

But the overall concept is nicey nice!

Edit: After deciding to look up Fuel-Flex, I realized I am a dumdum, and the ad isn't from the future. With that cleared up, I love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not sure how I feel. I like parts of it, but overall I don't know if I find it interesting enough to upvote. However, this is the second time today this has happened, so I'm going to no vote for now and read it again later, if it's still up by the time I get on tomorrow and I will vote/give a more detailed critique then.

E: Now that it's been cleaned up a bit and expanded, I like it. +1 on faith that it is original and the article's merit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well that's certainly one way to go about solving the fuel crisis…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Diction. The concept itself is interesting, but you didn't really do much with it. At first, the spores and virus seemed to be just some over-the-top dangerous infection thing with no purpose but to be dangerous. Then you brought in the idea that it had been designed to create easy and renewable petroleum, which could be have added a lot to this SCP if you had elaborated on that, and taken a few minutes to think about the implications of that.

This would mean that someone set out to create a virus that would make plants create petroleum, but accidentally made it virulent enough that it would spread out of control, and infect animals. That is one huge and highly convenient mistake right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't need to apologize! Does anyone else feel the same way about the ad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The writing is very good, the technical details are strong, and the concept is fine… but I'm not sure how interesting I find it. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like the concept, but the ad at the bottom feels tacked on, and the narrative hook is weak. sorry.
EDIT: I re-read this after you added the entries, and I like this slightly better. Changing from -1 to neutral vote.
